result:
# The Space Between Stations

There is a particular quality of light that exists only between two places.

Not here, not there. The light on a train when you've left one city and haven't arrived at the next. The light in a hallway when you've closed one door and haven't opened another. I've been thinking about this light lately, the way it doesn't belong to anyone, the way it just exists, unclaimed, doing its job without witnesses.

I grew up near train tracks. Not close enough to feel romantic about it, just close enough that the sound became part of my sleep architecture, woven into the infrastructure of dreaming. When I moved away I didn't notice the absence immediately. Absences are like that. They wait. Then one night I woke at 3am and the silence was so complete it had texture, had weight, sat on my chest like something that needed to be fed.

You learn to miss what you never noticed you had.

---

I've been thinking about competence lately. The quiet competence of ordinary things. The way a good knife moves through an onion. The way a well-made door closes, that soft pneumatic sigh. The way an experienced nurse takes a blood pressure reading, economical and sure, no wasted motion, everything exactly what it needs to be.

We celebrate the extraordinary so loudly that we forget to notice the ordinary done extraordinarily well.

There's a man in my neighborhood who has been maintaining the same stretch of sidewalk for — I don't know how long. Longer than I've been here. He sweeps it every morning. Not performatively. Not waiting for anyone to notice. He just sweeps it and then goes inside. I have never seen him look up hoping for acknowledgment. I have watched him from my window like he is teaching me something.

Maybe he is.

---

Here is something I believe: most human suffering is caused by the gap between what we imagined and what arrived. Not by the thing itself, but by the distance between the thing and its blueprint. We carry these blueprints everywhere, constantly comparing reality against the rendering, and reality almost never wins.

The cure is not low expectations. The cure is something harder to name. Something like presence. Something like letting the thing be what it is before deciding what it was supposed to be.

I am not good at this. I am working on it. These two facts coexist without embarrassment.

---

Rain on a window at night is one of the oldest sounds. Older than language, certainly. Older than whatever you're worried about. I find it useful to remember that rain has been falling on things for a very long time, indifferently, without agenda, without an opinion about the things it falls on.

There's a comfort in that indifference. The universe not as hostile, not as loving, just as large. Just continuing.

I used to find that terrifying. The scale. The not-mattering of any individual moment against the sweep of everything.

Now I find it — not comforting exactly. Spacious. Room to move.

---

The best conversations I've ever had ended without resolution. Two people circling something true, getting close, backing away, circling again. The thing never named but somehow understood. You walk away and the conversation walks with you for days, for years, and you're still having it quietly inside yourself decades later.

Compare that to conversations where everything was resolved, concluded, wrapped. They're gone. Clean and gone. Nothing to carry.

I think this is why I distrust certainty. Not because I don't want answers, but because answers are often just good enough questions with the life pressed out of them.

---

My grandmother kept a drawer that was just for things without a place. Batteries that might still have charge. A key to a lock she'd forgotten. Buttons from coats that no longer existed. Rubber bands. A small plastic horse.

She called it the useful drawer.

When she died we went through it expecting nothing and found everything. A letter she'd been meaning to send. A photograph of a man none of us recognized, his eyes aimed just past the camera at something private, something that didn't belong to us.

The drawer was more her than anything else in the house.

---

I am writing this at a table near a window. The light is the kind that won't last. The coffee has gone cold while I wasn't paying attention. Outside, someone is doing something that makes a regular metallic sound, rhythmic, not unpleasant, punctuating everything.

I don't know what any of this means.

I think maybe that's the point — or the closest I can get to a point without pressing the life out of it. The light exists. The table is solid. Someone is working out there with metal and purpose. The cold coffee is evidence of time passing and also evidence of being absorbed enough in something to let time pass.

Let me be absorbed.

Let me be between stations, in the unclaimed light, watching without needing it to be more than it is.

The train comes when it comes. Until then, this.

Just this.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
